name: types of scaffolding in education
top_issues: C9 — Standards alignment is conspicuously absent for an article about lesson design and differentiation. The article cites research but never names a single applicable framework (Common Core ELA standards on academic language, NGSS science practices, ISTE standards for student agency). For a piece aimed at K-12 teachers making instructional decisions, this is a real gap that undermines credibility with standards-conscious educators.

C10 — Wayground's value proposition is generic throughout. 'Wayground's standards-aligned library and built-in accommodations' appears twice but names no specific feature, workflow, or time saving that a teacher could act on. Contrast with what this could say: 'Wayground's Create with AI lets you generate a tiered version of any worksheet — sentence frames, vocabulary preview, and full text — in under two minutes.' The current framing reads like a banner ad, not a peer recommendation.

C2 — The opening two sentences ('Watch a student's face light up... That moment when...') are evocative but delay concrete value. A teacher scanning during a planning period needs the payoff sentence — 'There are six types of scaffolding, and knowing when to fade each one is what separates effective differentiation from dependency' — to come first, not third.
positive_signals: The comparison table is genuinely excellent — the 'Quick Check' and 'Fade-Out Move' columns are the kind of at-a-glance reference a teacher would photograph or print; this is the article's strongest asset.

The 'How to choose the right type' section with three diagnostic categories (understanding / execution / persistence) and observable student signals is highly practical and reflects real classroom decision-making, not theory.
narrative: This article is substantively strong — the six-type framework is well-explained, the comparison table is genuinely photocopy-worthy, and the FAQ section answers the questions teachers actually ask mid-unit rather than in a graduate seminar. However, two criteria score at 2 and trigger a mandatory REVISE: Wayground's product mentions are too vague to be persuasive ("standards-aligned library and built-in accommodations" says nothing a competitor couldn't claim), and the article never names a single applicable standard or framework despite covering lesson design and differentiation for K-12 classrooms — a credibility gap that standards-accountable teachers will notice. Fix those two elements — add one named Wayground feature with a concrete time-saving use case, and anchor at least one section to a named framework (NGSS practices, Common Core ELA anchor standards, or ISTE) — and this article clears the bar comfortably.
